candidate_problems.text: What are the minimal and jointly sufficient structural conditions under which decentralized informational mechanisms actually shift a system from one-shot defection to repeated cooperation, and can these conditions be formalized as threshold variables across memory persistence, observability, identity stability, signaling costliness, and sanction capacity?

How can one distinguish cases where 'better transparency' is genuinely payoff-changing from cases where it is merely descriptive and leaves equilibrium lock-in intact, especially in environments with non-local, delayed, anonymous, or irreversible harms?

What hybrid institutional designs optimally combine decentralized informational infrastructure with limited central enforcement when decentralized sanctions are too weak against one-shot extractive actors, cheap identity reset, or diffuse public harms?
